title: Interpret constant of proportionality in graphs
description: Interpret the meaning of constants of proportionality in graphs.
topic_path: Compare and interpret constants of proportionality
Interpret constant of proportionality in graphs

assessment_items.question_content:
Jameson collects eggs from his chickens every day. The graph shows the approximate number of eggs Jameson collects over a couple of days.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What does $4.5$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: The graph shows a proportional relationship between the number of days and the approximate number of eggs Jameson has collected.  Jameson collects about $4.5$ eggs in $1$ day.

It doesn't make sense to collect half of an egg, and the graph only gives approximate numbers.

It is more reasonable to say that Jameson collects about $4$ eggs or about $5$ eggs in $1$ day.
Jameson collects about $4$ or $5$ eggs per day.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The axes are marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 2, ranging from 2 to 8. The x-axis is labeled as Days and the y-axis is labeled as Eggs. The graph displays a single slant line starting from (0,0). The line passes through a point marked with a filled dot at (1,4.5) with an arrowhead at its end. The point is labeled as parenthesis open 1 comma 4.5 parenthesis closed.

choices.content: Jameson collects about $4$ or $5$ eggs per day.
It takes $4.5$ days for Jameson to collect an egg.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shows the relationship between mass of hamster food and the number of days it takes Gabin's hamster to eat it.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What does $30$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: The graph describes a proportional relationship between mass of hamster food and the number of days it takes Gabin's hamster to eat it. Gabin's hamster takes $1$ day to eat $30$ grams of food.
Each additional day increases the total amount of food by $30$ grams.
Gabin's hamster eats $30$ grams of food a day.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The x-axis is labeled as Days and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 1, ranging from 1 to 3. The y-axis is labeled as Grams and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 20, ranging from 20 to 80. The graph displays a single slant line starting from (0,0) with an arrowhead at its end. The line passes through a point marked with a filled dot at (1,30). The point is labeled as parenthesis open 1 comma 30 parenthesis closed.

choices.content: Gabin's hamster eats a gram of food every $30$ days.
Gabin feeds his hamster once every $30$ days.
Gabin's hamster eats $30$ grams of food a day.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shows the relationship between the volume of coffee in a cup and the length of time Cassie pours coffee into the cup.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What does $50$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: The graph describes a proportional relationship between the volume of coffee in a cup and the length of time Cassie pours coffee. It takes Cassie $1$ second to pour $50$ milliliters of coffee.
Each additional second increases the volume of coffee Cassie pours by $50$ milliliters.
Cassie pours coffee at a rate of $50$ milliliters per second.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The x-axis is labeled as seconds and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 1, ranging from 1 to 3. The y-axis is labeled as milliliters and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 50, ranging from 50 to 200. The graph displays a single slant line starting from (0,0) with an arrowhead at its end. The line passes through a point marked with a filled dot at (1,50). The point is labeled as parenthesis open 1 comma 50 parenthesis closed.

choices.content: Cassie pours coffee at a rate of $50$ milliliters per second.
The cup holds up to $50$ milliliters of coffee.
Cassie can pour $50$ cups of coffee in a second.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shows the relationship between the length of time Ted spends knitting and the number of scarves he knits.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What does $16$ mean in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints: The graph describes a proportional relationship between the number of hours Ted spends knitting and the number of scarves he knits. To finish $1$ scarf, Ted spends $16$ hours knitting.
Each additional scarf increases the length of time Ted spends knitting by $16$ hours.
It takes Ted $16$ hours to knit a scarf.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The x-axis is labeled as scarves and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 1, ranging from 1 to 4. The y-axis is labeled as hours and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 20, ranging from 20 to 80. The graph displays a single slant line starting from (0,0) with an arrowhead at its end. The line passes through a point marked with a filled dot at (1,16). The point is labeled as parenthesis open 1 comma 16 parenthesis closed.

choices.content: Ted knits $16$ centimeters of scarf in an hour.
It takes Ted $16$ hours to knit a scarf.

assessment_items.question_content:
Harlan makes a snack mix with proportional amounts of cereal and popcorn. The graph shows how much popcorn he uses with different amounts of cereal to make the snack mix.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many cups of popcorn does Harlan use per cup of cereal?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] cups

**How many cups of cereal does Harlan use per cup of popcorn?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] cups
hints:
We can use a point from the graph to figure out the relationship between cups of popcorn and cups of cereal.

[[☃ image 1]]

Using the point $(1,2)$ we see that when there are $1$ cup of cereal, Harlan uses $2$ cups of popcorn. Harlan uses $2$ times as much popcorn as cereal.

We can write this relationship as a verbal equation.

$\maroonD{\text{[cups of popcorn]}}=2\cdot\greenD{\text{[cups of cereal]}}$

Let's substitute in $\greenD{1}$ cup of cereal and solve for how much popcorn Harlan uses.

$\begin{align}\maroonD{\text{[cups of popcorn]}}&=2\cdot\greenD{1}\\\\
&=2
\end{align}$

Harlan uses $2$ cups of popcorn per $1$ cup of cereal.

For the second question, we know Harlan uses $\maroonD{1}$ cup of popcorn, but we do not know the number of cups of cereal. We'll substitute into our equation again.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{\text{[cups of popcorn]}}&=2\cdot\greenD{\text{cups of cereal}}\\\\
\maroonD{1}&=2\cdot\greenD{\text{cups of cereal}}\\\\
\dfrac{1}{2}\cdot \maroonD{1}&=\dfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2\cdot\greenD{\text{cups of cereal}}\\\\
\dfrac{1}{2}&=\greenD{\text{cups of cereal}}
\end{align}$

Harlan uses $\dfrac{1}{2}$ cup of cereal per cup of popcorn.

This makes sense, because the graph shows that when Harlan uses $1$ cup of popcorn, he uses between $0$ and $1$ cups of cereal.

[[☃ image 1]]

Harlan uses $2$ cups of popcorn per cup of cereal.

Harlan uses $\dfrac{1}{2}$ cup of cereal per cup of popcorn.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The axes are marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 2, ranging from 2 to 8. The x-axis is labeled as Cereal (cups) and the y-axis is labeled as Popcorn (cups). The graph displays a single slant line starting from (0,0) with an arrowhead at its end. The line passes through a point marked with a filled dot at (1,2).

assessment_items.question_content:
Clark has a budget of $\$12$ to spend to buy nuts for a party. The three lines represent the cost of buying bags of three kinds of nuts (walnuts, pecans, or peanuts) of varying weights.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which kind of nut can Clark buy the most of with his $\$12$ budget?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The cost of each kind of nut increases at a constant rate.

The kind of nut that Clark can buy the most of with his $\$12$ budget will be the same as the kind of nut he can buy the most of with a smaller budget.

For $\$4$, Clark can buy a little less than ${0.5}$ kilograms of $\blueD{\text{walnuts}}$, or exactly $0.5$ kilograms of $\greenD{\text{pecans}}$, or $1$ kilogram of peanuts.

He can buy the most peanuts with $\$4$, so the same will be true if we multiply his budget by $3$.
Clark can buy the most peanuts with his $\$12$ budget.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The x-axis is labeled as Kilograms and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 0.5, ranging from 0.5 to 2. The y-axis is labeled as Cost (dollars) and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 2, ranging from 2 to 8. The graph displays three slant lines labeled Walnuts, Pecans, Peanuts clockwise. The lines start from (0,0) and have an arrowhead at the end. Line Peanuts is in the middle of the graph. Line Walnuts is close to the x-axis. Line Pecans is between Line Walnuts and Line Peanuts, very closer to the Line Walnuts.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bao is mixing flour and water to make dough. The graph shows how much water he uses for different amounts of flour.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many liters of water does Bao use per liter of flour?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] liters

**How many liters of flour does Bao use per liter of water?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] liters
hints:
We can use a point from the graph to figure out the relationship between liters of water and liters of flour.

[[☃ image 1]]

Using the point $(3,1)$, we see that when there are $3$ liters of flour, Bao uses $1$ liter of water. Bao uses $\dfrac{1}{3}$ as much water as flour.

We can write this relationship as a verbal equation.

$\maroonD{\text{[liters of water]}}=\dfrac{1}{3}\cdot\greenD{\text{[liters of flour]}}$

Let's substitute in $\greenD{1}$ liter of flour and solve for how much water Bao uses.

$\begin{align}\maroonD{\text{[liters of water]}}&=\dfrac{1}{3}\cdot\greenD{1}\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{3}
\end{align}$

Bao uses $\dfrac{1}{3}$ liter of water per $1$ liter of flour.

The point $(\greenD{3},\maroonD{1})$ satisfies the verbal equation:

$\maroonD{1}=\dfrac{1}{3}\cdot\greenD{3}$

Bao uses $3$ liters of flour per liter of water.

Bao uses $\dfrac{1}{3}$ liter of water per liter of flour.

Bao uses $3$ liters of flour per liter of water.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The axes are marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 1, ranging from 1 to 4. The x-axis is labeled as Flour (liters) and the y-axis is labeled as Water (liters). The graph displays a single slant line starting from (0,0) with an arrowhead at its end. The line passes through a point marked with a filled dot at (3,1).

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shows the distance Kendrick walks in different lengths of time.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many kilometers does Kendrick walk per hour?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] kilometers

**How long does it take Kendrick to walk $1$ kilometer?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] hours
hints:
We can use a point from the graph to figure out the relationship between distance and time walked.


[[☃ image 1]]

Using the point $(1,5)$, we see that Kendrick walks $5$ kilometers in $1$ hour.

We can write this relationship as a verbal equation.

$\maroonD{\text{[kilometers]}}=5\cdot\greenD{\text{[hours]}}$

The point $(\greenD{1},\maroonD{5})$ satisfies the verbal equation:

$\maroonD{5}=5\cdot\greenD{1}$

Kendrick walks $5$ kilometers per hour.

For the second question, we know Kendrick walks $\maroonD{1}$ kilometer, but we do not know the number of hours. We'll substitute into our equation again.

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{\text{[kilometers]}}&=5\cdot\greenD{\text{hours}}\\\\
\maroonD{1}&=5\cdot\greenD{\text{hours}}\\\\
\dfrac{1}{5}\cdot \maroonD{1}&=\dfrac{1}{5}\cdot 5\cdot\greenD{\text{hours}}\\\\
\dfrac{1}{5}&=\greenD{\text{hours}}
\end{align}$

It takes Kendrick $\dfrac{1}{5}$ hour to walk $1$ kilometer.

Kendrick walks $5$ kilometers per hour.

It take Kendrick $\dfrac{1}{5}$ hour to walk $1$ kilometer.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The x-axis is labeled as hours and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 1, ranging from 1 to 4. The y-axis is labeled as kilometers and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 5, ranging from 5 to 15. The graph displays a single slant line starting from (0,0) with an arrowhead at its end. The line passes through a point marked with a filled dot at (1,5).

assessment_items.question_content:
The three lines represent the amount of water, over time, in three tanks that are the same size.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which tank is filling most quickly?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
All three tanks start out empty and start being filled at the same time. Each tank is being filled at a constant rate.

The tank that is filling most quickly will have the most water in it after the same amount of time goes by.
After $2$ minutes, tank $\blueD{A}$ has $7$ gallons of water, tank $\greenD{B}$ has $2$ gallons of water, and tank $\purpleD{C}$ has less than $1$ gallon of water.
Tank $\blueD{A}$ is filling most quickly.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The axes are marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 2, ranging from 2 to 8. The x-axis is labeled as Minutes and the y-axis is labeled as Water (gallons). The graph displays three slant lines labeled A, B, C clockwise. The lines start from (0,0) and have an arrowhead at the end. Line B is in the middle of the graph. Line A is close to the y-axis. Line C is close to the x-axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
Satoko has $30$ tokens to spend at an arcade. The three lines represent the cost of playing Satoko's three favorite games for various numbers of times.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which game can Satoko play the most number of times with her tokens?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The graph shows that the cost of each game increases at a constant rate.

No matter how many tokens Satoko has, the game she can play the most times will not change.

For $10$ tokens, Satoko can play $\blueD{\text{Claw}}$ $2$ times, $\greenD{\text{DDR}}$ $3$ times, and $\purpleC{\text{KOF}}$ $5$ times.

She can play $\text{KOF}$ the most number of times with $10$ tokens, so the same will be true if we multiply the her number of tokens by $3$.
Satoko can play KOF the most number of times with her tokens.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The x-axis is labeled as Times and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 1, ranging from 1 to 9. The y-axis is labeled as Cost (tokens) and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 5, ranging from 5 to 25.The graph displays three slant lines labeled Claw, DDR, KOF clockwise. The lines start from (0,0) and have an arrowhead at the end. Line DDR is in the middle of the graph and between Line Claw and Line KOF. Line KOF is close to the x-axis. Line Claw is away from the y-axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
The three lines represent the price, in dollars, of three different brands of chocolate bars.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which brand of chocolate bar is the most expensive?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Each brand of chocolate bar has a constant price per bar.

The most expensive brand of chocolate bar will have the highest price for the same number of bars.
For $5$ chocolate bars, the price of brand $\blueD{X}$ is $\$25$, the price of brand $\greenD{Y}$ is $\$20$, and the price of brand $\purpleC{Z}$ is $\$30$.
Brand $\purpleC{Z}$ is the most expensive.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The x-axis is labeled as Bars and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 2, ranging from 2 to 8. The y-axis is labeled as Price (dollars) and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 10, ranging from 10 to 40.The graph displays three slant lines labeled Z, X, Y clockwise. The lines start from (0,0) and have an arrowhead at the end. Line X is in the middle of the graph and between Line Z and Line Y. Line Z is very close to Line X and away from the y-axis. Line Y is close to the x-axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
The three lines represent the distance traveled, in kilometers, by three cyclists.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which cyclist is the slowest?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
All three cyclists start at $0$ kilometers. Each cyclist travels at a constant speed.

The slowest cyclist will travel the shortest distance after the same amount of time goes by.
After $3$ hours, cyclist $\blueD{M}$ traveled almost $50$ kilometers, cyclist $\greenD{N}$ traveled more than $70$ kilometers, and cyclist $\purpleC{O}$ traveled less than $40$ kilometers.
Cyclist $\purpleC{O}$ is the slowest.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The x-axis is labeled as Hours and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 1, ranging from 1 to 5. The y-axis is labeled as Kilometers and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 20, ranging from 20 to 100.The graph displays three slant lines labeled N, M, O clockwise. The lines start from (0,0) and have an arrowhead at the end. Line N is away from the y-axis. Line O is closer to the x-axis. Line M is in the middle of Line N and Line O, closer to Line O.

assessment_items.question_content:
The three lines represent the number of hot dogs eaten by three contestants at a hot dog eating competition.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which contestant was eating at the slowest rate?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
All three contestant start eating at the same time. They each eat at a constant rate.

The slowest contestant will have eaten the fewest hot dogs after the same amount of time goes by.
After $4$ minutes, contestant $\blueD{J}$ ate almost $20$ hot dogs, contestant $\greenD{K}$ ate around $15$ hot dogs, and contestant $\purpleC{L}$ ate over $20$ hot dogs.
Contestant $\greenD{K}$ was eating at the slowest rate.

options.alt: A graph on a Cartesian coordinate system with x and y-axes. The x-axis is labeled as Minutes and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 1, ranging from 1 to 6. The y-axis is labeled as Hot dogs and marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 10, ranging from 10 to 30. The graph displays three slant lines labeled L, J, K clockwise. The lines start from (0,0) and have an arrowhead at the end. Line L is in the middle of the graph. Line K is close to the x-axis. Line J is between Line L and Line K.
